description: This weak-beam dark-field (WBDF) transmission electron micrograph displays a dissociated dislocation in the base Fe-Ni-Cr austenitic steel (Fe-15Ni-11Cr base composition), serving as the representative imaging condition for measuring partial dislocation separation to determine stacking fault energy (SFE). The micrograph shows two partial dislocations separated by distance d (indicated by double-headed arrow), with annotations defining α as the angle between the dislocation line vector and the Burgers vector b of the undissociated dislocation, and the diffraction vector g parallel to b (g//b two-beam condition). The top-right inset presents the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. With a 50 nm scale bar, this image exemplifies the methodology where multiple (d, α) data pairs were recorded along several dislocations and input into isotropic elasticity theory equations to calculate the base alloy SFE of 26.3 ± 3.0 mJ m⁻². The figure establishes the experimental basis for comparing SFE variations between the base alloy and its 2.5 wt.% Si and 2.5 wt.% Al modified counterparts, demonstrating that Si decreases SFE while Al increases it.

Figure 2 presents a scatter plot illustrating the dependence of the austenite lattice parameter on silicon and aluminum alloying in Fe-Ni-Cr-based austenitic steels. The y-axis displays the Austenite Lattice Parameter in nanometers (nm), ranging from approximately 0.358 to 0.360 nm, while the x-axis indicates Relative Alloying content in weight percent (wt pct) from 0 to 3. Three distinct data points are shown: the Base alloy (Fe-15Ni-11Cr) at 0 wt pct with a lattice parameter of approximately 0.3585 nm (circular symbol), the 2.5 Si alloy at 2.5 wt pct with approximately 0.3587 nm (triangular symbol), and the 2.5 Al alloy at 2.5 wt pct with approximately 0.3598 nm (square symbol). The plot demonstrates that 2.5 wt pct Al addition significantly increases the austenite lattice parameter by approximately 0.0013 nm relative to the base alloy, whereas 2.5 wt pct Si exerts only a minimal effect, increasing the parameter by roughly 0.0002 nm. This data supports the conclusion that Al substantially expands the austenite lattice while Si has negligible influence on lattice dimensions.

Figure 3 comprises two scatter plots illustrating the influence of silicon and aluminum alloying on stacking fault energy (SFE) in Fe-Ni-Cr austenitic steels. Subfigure (a) displays partial dislocation separation distance (in nm) versus perfect dislocation character angle (0–90 degrees) for three alloys: Base (Fe-15Ni-11Cr), 2.5 wt.% Si, and 2.5 wt.% Al. Measured TEM data points are represented by distinct symbols—upward triangles for 2.5 Si, circles for Base, and squares for 2.5 Al—with solid curves indicating theoretical trends calculated from average SFE values and dashed curves denoting upper and lower bounds based on standard deviations. The analysis yields SFE values of 19.9 ± 2.0 mJ/m² for the 2.5 Si alloy, 26.3 ± 3.0 mJ/m² for the Base alloy, and 38.3 ± 6.1 mJ/m² for the 2.5 Al alloy. Subfigure (b) presents the direct dependence of SFE on relative alloying content, revealing that 2.5 wt.% Al increases SFE by approximately 12.0 mJ/m² from the base value, while 2.5 wt.% Si decreases it by approximately 6.4 mJ/m². These results demonstrate that Al additions significantly raise SFE whereas Si additions reduce it, thereby influencing deformation mechanisms and austenite stability in metastable austenitic steels.
